summary: Jeff Atwood shares Seth Godin's framework for building effective websites, emphasizing that successful sites focus on converting strangers to friends to customers through persuasive storytelling. The post highlights that websites can only produce four meaningful outcomes: directing users elsewhere, capturing permission to follow up, making sales, or generating word-of-mouth. Atwood endorses this focused, no-nonsense approach to web design that avoids marketing gimmicks. The brief post also includes a practical software recommendation for PDF reading.
key_points: Effective websites serve two core purposes: converting strangers to friends to customers, and telling a persuasive story in the right tone
There are only four meaningful user actions on a website: navigate to another page you want, give permission for follow-up, buy something, or tell a friend
Any website features beyond these four outcomes waste time, money, and focus
Good web design means being effective without resorting to marketing sleaze
money_quotes: how to build a popular website without becoming a marketing weasel in the process
Turn a stranger into a friend, and a friend into a customer
Talk in a tone of voice that persuades people to believe the story you're telling
If your site is attempting to do more than this, you're wasting time and money and, more important, focus
key_insight: Websites should ruthlessly focus on only four possible user outcomes—navigation, permission capture, purchase, or word-of-mouth—and anything beyond that is wasted effort.
